title: Open a CSV in QGIS
excerpt: Import tabular data with coordinates and turn it into a mappable layer you can explore and style.
content:
## What this covers

CSV files can become map-ready layers when they include columns for longitude and latitude. This tutorial covers the import process using QGIS's Delimited Text workflow.

## Before you begin

Open the CSV file in a spreadsheet application first and confirm it contains coordinate columns. Look for fields named longitude, latitude, lon, lat, x, y, or similar. If the file contains only names or identifiers without coordinates, it is not yet mappable as a spatial layer.

## Steps

1. Open **Data Source Manager** from the Layer menu or press Ctrl+L.
2. Select **Delimited Text** from the left panel.
3. Click the browse button and select your CSV file.
4. Under **Geometry Definition**, confirm the X field maps to your longitude column and the Y field maps to your latitude column.
5. Set the **Geometry CRS** to EPSG:4326 if your coordinates are in decimal degrees (standard latitude/longitude).
6. Click **Add**.

## After loading

- Zoom to the layer to confirm points appear in the expected location.
- Open the attribute table to inspect the associated data.
- If points appear in the wrong location, double-check that the X and Y field assignments are not reversed.

## Note

If your CSV contains place names but no coordinates, you will need to geocode the data before it can be displayed as a spatial layer. This is a separate workflow beyond basic CSV import.
